original_hypothesis.title: Reentrancy in Core minting via ERC721Receiver callback may allow unexpected nested mints/airdrops and break supply/allowance assumptions
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: NextGenCore._mintProcessing uses _safeMint, which triggers onERC721Received on contract recipients. During this callback, the recipient can call back into other public Core functions (e.g., burn) or external Minter functions that might be callable, potentially creating nested state transitions while the mint flow is in-progress. Combined with the already-fragile circulationSupply accounting and external randomizer calls, this could lead to inconsistent mint limits or supply corruption.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete reentrancy surface because _safeMint is an external callback into an untrusted contract mid-mint. While mint() itself is only callable by minterContract, the callback can still interact with other public entry points (burn, metadata views) and may interact with the Minter contract (mint/burnToMint) if any path permits.
deployment_assumptions: MinterContract.mint allows _mintTo to be an arbitrary contract that implements onERC721Received
There exists at least one callable path during callback that can cause additional mints/airdrops/burn-to-mints in the same transaction (directly or indirectly)
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No production wiring evidence beyond test fixtures; mint() allows arbitrary _mintTo; Core uses _safeMint callbacks by design.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _mintProcessing calls _safeMint (external callback) after writing tokenData and tokenIdsToCollectionIds and after calling external randomizer.calculateTokenHash

preconditions: Recipient _mintTo is a contract implementing onERC721Received
The callback reenters into some state-changing function(s) on Core/Minter/Randomizer that are reachable with current msg.sender
Any reentered function sequence creates an observable accounting or state violation (supply, mint limits, token data)

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: NextGenCore.sol _mintProcessing uses _safeMint with no nonReentrant guard; external call to randomizer happens before _safeMint
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: external callback during sensitive state transition
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Nested calls could exploit missing atomic assumptions (e.g., supply counters, per-address limits tracked in Core)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No reentrancy guard; no explicit minting lock; no checks-effects-interactions discipline around callback

analysis_questions: During onERC721Received, can the recipient call MinterContract.mint/burnToMint/burnOrSwapExternalToMint in a way that passes checks (msg.sender changes)?
Can the recipient call Core.burn on the just-minted token within the callback and create inconsistent burnAmount/totalSupplyOfCollection behavior?
Do any functions rely on intermediate state that could be observed/manipulated mid-mint?
validation_requirements: Build ERC721Receiver that reenters into Core.burn and any reachable Minter function; fuzz sequences
Assert invariants: circulationSupply vs minted count, burnAmount correctness, per-address mint counts
Check if any privileged checks mistakenly allow callback contract to escalate actions

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker mints to a malicious ERC721Receiver contract. In onERC721Received, it immediately calls Core.burn on the received token and/or triggers further mint flows (if reachable), attempting to desynchronize accounting and create phantom supply/burn states that block others or skew pricing.
prerequisites: Ability to mint at least one token to a contract address

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: High: could brick collection minting, bypass intended per-address limits, or corrupt supply/burn accounting
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All minters in affected collection; marketplace users relying on scarcity/supply reporting

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Maybe (reentrant depth and gas stipend during callback)
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Track nested call traces and state deltas within one transaction

validation_result.mechanism: reentrancy via _safeMint callback bypasses per-address mint limits
validation_result.impact_summary: A malicious contract recipient can reenter MinterContract.mint() during the onERC721Received callback, bypassing per-address mint limits because the per-address counter is updated AFTER _safeMint returns. Burn during callback is also possible.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The _mintProcessing function in NextGenCore.sol calls _safeMint, which triggers an onERC721Received callback on contract recipients. Critically, in NextGenCore.mint(), the per-address mint counter (tokensMintedPerAddress or tokensMintedAllowlistAddress) is updated AFTER _mintProcessing (and thus AFTER the _safeMint callback). During the callback, the recipient contract can call MinterContract.mint() again. Since the per-address counter has not yet been incremented from the outer call, the MinterContract's check `require(gencore.retrieveTokensMintedPublicPerAddress(col, msg.sender) + _numberOfTokens <= gencore.viewMaxAllowance(col))` sees a stale value and passes. The nested mint succeeds, effectively bypassing the per-address mint limit. Additionally, the burn() function on NextGenCore is public and only requires _isApprovedOrOwner, which the callback recipient satisfies. Burning during the callback is also possible, creating a state where the per-address counter is incremented for a non-existent token. No reentrancy guard (nonReentrant modifier) exists on any of these functions.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal correctly identifies _mintProcessing at the indicated line range using _safeMint with no reentrancy guard. The critical code defect is the ordering: gencore.mint() calls _mintProcessing → _safeMint → callback BEFORE updating tokensMintedPerAddress/tokensMintedAllowlistAddress. MinterContract.mint() also has no reentrancy guard, making the reentrant call possible.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Per-address mint counter (tokensMintedPerAddress/tokensMintedAllowlistAddress) is updated AFTER _mintProcessing, which contains the _safeMint callback. During the callback, a reentrant call reads the stale counter value.
_safeMint triggers onERC721Received on contract recipients, creating a callback vector. No reentrancy guard protects this function.
No reentrancy guard on MinterContract.mint(). The per-address check reads from gencore which has stale state during a reentrant call from within _safeMint callback.

Public function callable during _safeMint callback. The recipient contract owns the just-minted token and can burn it before the mint flow completes, incrementing burnAmount while the per-address counter still gets incremented afterward.

calling_sequence: 1. AttackerContract calls MinterContract.mint(_collectionID, 1, ..., AttackerContract, ...) with sufficient ETH during public phase
2. MinterContract checks: retrieveTokensMintedPublicPerAddress(col, AttackerContract) + 1 <= maxAllowance → passes (count is 0, maxAllowance is e.g. 2)
5. _mintProcessing: sets tokenData, calls randomizer.calculateTokenHash, sets tokenIdsToCollectionIds, calls _safeMint(AttackerContract, mintIndex)
6. _safeMint mints the token, then calls AttackerContract.onERC721Received()
7. In onERC721Received, AttackerContract calls MinterContract.mint() again with _mintTo = AttackerContract and fresh ETH
8. MinterContract checks: retrieveTokensMintedPublicPerAddress(col, AttackerContract) + 1 <= maxAllowance → STILL PASSES (count is still 0, not yet updated from step 3)
9. Nested gencore.mint succeeds, incrementing supply and eventually incrementing tokensMintedPerAddress to 1
10. Callback returns to outer _safeMint which returns to outer gencore.mint
12. Result: AttackerContract minted 2 tokens but the per-address check only saw count=0 for both, when the second should have seen count=1
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The checks-effects-interactions violation is in NextGenCore.mint() at lines 169-182: _mintProcessing (containing the external _safeMint callback) is called BEFORE the per-address counter update. MinterContract.mint() at line 232-233 checks the counter via gencore.retrieveTokensMintedPublicPerAddress() which reads stale state during reentrancy. Neither contract has a reentrancy guard.

deployment_assumptions_checked: MinterContract.mint allows _mintTo to be an arbitrary contract that implements onERC721Received
There exists at least one callable path during callback that can cause additional mints
config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment script (fixturesDeployment.js) deploys MinterContract with gencore and delegation contracts. The _mintTo parameter in MinterContract.mint is user-controlled with no restriction to EOA-only. The NXT randomizer is used in test fixtures, which is synchronous (doesn't block the mint flow). No reentrancy guards are deployed or configured anywhere.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack is realistic and requires low capital (just the mint cost). Attacker deploys a contract implementing IERC721Receiver that, in onERC721Received, calls MinterContract.mint() again for the same collection with _mintTo = itself. Since tokensMintedPerAddress has not been updated from the outer call yet, the reentrant call's per-address check passes with stale data. This allows minting beyond the per-address limit. For the burn scenario: the attacker's contract can also call NextGenCore.burn() during the callback to burn the just-received token, creating a state where collectionCirculationSupply is permanently incremented with no corresponding token, and the per-address counter still gets incremented afterward.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH impact: Per-address mint limits can be bypassed, allowing a single address to mint more than its allowed quota. This undermines collection fairness and scarcity guarantees. For collections with per-address limits (maxCollectionPurchases), an attacker can accumulate more tokens than intended. The burn-during-callback path also creates accounting inconsistencies (per-address counter tracks a non-existent token). Combined with the phantom supply increment bug (circulationSupply incremented before conditional mint), the reentrancy could also lead to supply corruption.
recommended_mitigations: Add OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard (nonReentrant modifier) to NextGenCore.mint(), NextGenCore.airDropTokens(), NextGenCore.burnToMint(), and NextGenCore.burn()
Also add nonReentrant to MinterContract.mint(), MinterContract.burnToMint(), MinterContract.burnOrSwapExternalToMint(), and MinterContract.airDropTokens()
Follow checks-effects-interactions pattern: update tokensMintedPerAddress/tokensMintedAllowlistAddress BEFORE calling _mintProcessing (which contains the external callback)
Consider using _mint() instead of _safeMint() if ERC721Receiver callback is not required for the use case
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy full system (Core, Minter, Admins, NXT Randomizer, XRandoms). 2. Create collection with maxCollectionPurchases=2, totalSupply=10. 3. Set up minting phases and costs. 4. Deploy AttackerContract implementing IERC721Receiver that on first onERC721Received callback calls MinterContract.mint() with _mintTo=address(this). 5. From AttackerContract, call MinterContract.mint() with _numberOfTokens=1, _mintTo=address(this). 6. Assert that AttackerContract holds 2 tokens despite maxCollectionPurchases=2 and only one legitimate mint should have been allowed if it started with 1. 7. For burn test: modify callback to call NextGenCore.burn() on the received token. Assert burnAmount incremented and tokensMintedPerAddress also incremented for a non-existent token.
uncertainties: Gas cost of reentrant calls may limit depth of exploitation, but even one level of reentrancy is sufficient to bypass limits
Whether the onERC721Received callback has enough gas stipend for the full reentrant mint flow (likely yes, as _safeMint forwards all available gas)
additional_testing_needed: Fuzz testing with varying reentrancy depths to determine maximum exploitable mint count
Test interaction between reentrancy and salesOption 3 (1 mint per period) to see if timing checks can also be bypassed
Test allowlist phase reentrancy where Merkle proof is reused in the reentrant call
Test burnToMint reentrancy path (also uses _safeMint and has similar counter-update ordering)
